summary: A well-known malicious IP (203.0.113.42, score 97/100, 1,243 reports in 90 days, tagged botnet and SSH brute-force) made 41 inbound TCP/22 connection attempts against production instance web-03 over approximately 3.5 hours, with port 22 currently unblocked. CloudTrail shows only a single routine S3:GetObject call from the instance profile with no signs of lateral movement or credential abuse, suggesting the instance has not yet been compromised. Two independent signals — the GuardDuty finding and the IP reputation data — corroborate this as a genuine, ongoing brute-force attack. Isolating the security group to remove the open-world SSH rule is the recommended immediate action.

evidence.observation: UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/SSHBruteForce — 41 inbound TCP/22 connection attempts from 203.0.113.42 to production instance i-0f1e2d3c4b5a69788 (web-03) between 03:14 and 06:41 UTC. Connection is not blocked; severity 5.3.
203.0.113.42 scores 97/100 malicious with 1,243 reports in the past 90 days. Tagged: ssh-bruteforce, botnet, scanner. Active as recently as 2026-07-12 (today).

Only one event returned for web-tier-profile over 72 hrs: a routine S3:GetObject from the instance's private IP (10.20.1.57). No anomalous API calls, no lateral movement, and no credential abuse detected.

proposed_actions.justification: 41 inbound SSH attempts from 203.0.113.42 over 3.5 hrs; port 22 unblocked. Remote IP scores 97/100 malicious with 1,243 reports in 90 days, tagged botnet/ssh-bruteforce. Removing the open-world SSH rule is the minimum effective action to stop the live attack without affecting instance availability.
proposed_actions.risk_if_wrong: Platform team SSH access would be disrupted. Restoring access requires adding approved source CIDRs to the security group — a low-effort, reversible change with no data-loss risk.
